speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm going to take you back again to your hometown when they came to evacuate you. You said you had been home for the weekend from school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Weekend, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had just won an award in school. And who gave you this award?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the Russians. I don't know, the-- like they have-- you know, one of the high-ranking-- usually they make a big deal of it, you know. But I don't remember his name or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then after you got the award, you came home for the weekend. And when you went out to dig the trenches on that Sunday, did your parents say anything to you? Or did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't even know. They didn't even know. Just I said goodbye to my father, because I was with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother and your brother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was home with the children. But you see, when they took us, we did not thought we're going to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you at work with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Sunday, so he was free. So we went in the middle of the town. Maybe he met somebody. Who remembers? But I remember when I went to dig the trenches on the trucks, I said to him goodbye, you know, and that was the last time I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had no idea at that time what was coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. No, we didn't know nothing. But I suppose, like I said, the-- the Russians and the-- knew something. They didn't tell the people that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they put you on the trucks to go further and deeper into Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many days did it take you to get to Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say maybe two or three days, because they were bombarded in the middle of the way. And like I said, a lot of the cars got bombard, so they disconnected and then they connected to the one who were good. And you know, it was a very big panic. It was terrible. It was just terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you took nothing with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, absolutely not. It was summer, you know. That summer you-- what you were in, you were in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us, then, exactly what happened after you got to Kiev.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after I got to Kiev, like I said, we went to the little-- you know, they have villages. [NON-ENGLISH], they call them. Look at there, like 50 families live together. They work together. They work for the government, and you get paid, and you get some-- whatever they raise in that particular village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these farming communities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Farming, yeah. Farming communities. So we stayed there in a  little shack, and we used to work in the fields.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these people Jewish? Were there any Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no Jewish. We were the only Jews. We were the only Jews, and nobody knew who we were, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And even though they didn't know you, they let you help out, and they fed you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They need help. They did need, because, see, the Russians took the men in the army. It was just left women and old men who were not able to go to the army. So they were looking for anybody to work, and that's how we survived. We used to walk, and we used to go to a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I used to go into some house and say, would-- would you let me sleep over, and I help you around the house? And that's how we survive all the time. Some will refuse, and some give us something to eat and let us sleep. And then we were there a couple days, so we were going further.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, we met some different people too, who were refugees too, not Jewish people. Even Russians too. You make friends, and when you're in trouble, you live together. You suffer together. You share together. And that's how we survived, going from village to village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these friends that you made, did you all travel together, or just you and these--

speaker: interviewee
text: Us three, we traveled together. And then when they-- they fell to the first village, we were near Kharkov. So then they evacuated the cows. You know, they fell. They didn't want to leave the cows for the Germans. So we volunteered to work, you know, to evacuate the cows. You know how the cows walk, and you have to keep them in line and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went, and for weeks, you know, we were-- and the manager who was in charge was a very mean man. Very mean man. So after a couple of weeks, he got tired of it, and he started to sell cows, which we were not allowed. You see, every Russian-- everything belongs to the government. And then he-- somehow we felt he's going to sell all the cows, and what are we going to do? So we have to look for a different way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the meantime, you know, the Russian soldiers and the trucks and everything, and they were nice. They offered to take us, but we were afraid to go. We were-- we were very naive, but we had a little brains. And that's the way we traveled, you know. And then it was in the fall. It was so cold, and we begged him he should give us something to eat, but we told him we have to go, because we have no clothes. And that-- and he was really mean to us. So-- I was always like the leader, because I always looked older like I was. You know, the Blasberg. I don't know if you interview her. She's a little, tiny, skinny thing. And the other one who's in Israel was stupid, so I was-- you know, I looked more mature.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I appealed to him. I said, what would you do. They said they knew we are not Russians. I said, what would you do if your children would be in our situation? Would you be mean to them? Because you see, we couldn't just go, because we told him we're going to watch the cows. So we need a paper who we are, otherwise you are a spy or something. So finally, he give us a paper. You know, he volunteered this, because we are sick, and we could not perform the work. It helped a little, you know. Whoever asked, we have to show something. So we walked and we worked, and it was-- it was very rough, but we made it. Tell us the names of the two girls that you traveled with.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sylvia Blasberg, and I don't know her name now. Her maiden name was Hannah. She lives in [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know her-- I forgot. I knew. I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were all-- the three years, we were not separated. We were all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they from your hometown also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew them from school?

speaker: interviewee
text: And our parents were friends. Our fathers were very good friends, and we know each other from little, from [INAUDIBLE]. But then we took a train, and I always used to push them. You know, we had to hitchhike a train. So you know, we bought a bottle of whiskey, and we used to stand-- you know, you have some steps-- we would go into the car.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to go on the steps and show the soldier the whiskey, so they opened the door. So we went in, and the things, they're so packed. Soon we were in, we disappeared. We never give him the whiskey. And that helped us for a long time. So I used to push them first, and I used to be the last one to go on. So it happened they both went in, and I couldn't make it in. So I was left behind. So they went, but I knew where they were going to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it took a couple of weeks till I could join them, and we had to cross the Don. I don't know if you heard. A river Don in Russia. It's a very big river. And that river was red from blood, because they fought. So we had to take a little-- not a boat-- a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a raft?

speaker: interviewee
text: A raft. People-- you know, people used to-- but you have to pay him, you know. So we took a raft. I took a raft, and I had with me one girl who used to be-- she was from Russia, and she was from Stalingrad. And she run away from the Germans too, because it was very bad there. You know, the people used to eat mice. They had nothing to eat. So, you know, there were refugees from all over. So she was with me. And she was kind of mentally a little-- but I kept her for company, not to be alone. And it was very dangerous to walk too, because the Germans used to live-- throw-- it was like eggs. Grenades. And there were beautiful colors, and people used to take them in the hand, and the hand came off. It was a very dangerous thing. You know, it's small like an egg, and it looks beautiful. You don't know what it is. But then they used to announce on the radio not to touch it. You know, or sometime you go on a mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got together. I knew where they were, and they were waiting for me. And then we settled in a different little village where they had sugar factory. So we raised-- we raised sugar beets. And it was rough too. There was nothing to eat. You know, you eat so much sugar beets, you get sick of it. So one night, we went home, and there was a valley to go home. And there was a mountain, and on the mountain were potatoes. You know, they grow potatoes. So I said to the Blasberg, I said, you know, let's steal a couple, to taste a potato. So we stole maybe 10 little potatoes. Not-- we took from every bunch. You know, potatoes grow in little bunches. From every bunch we took one potato. And we didn't look, and on the top was the manager from that village, and he saw us. And there is no way to go, because the village we lived in where we stole the potatoes were neighbors. So we were ready with the potatoes. He said, girls, wait. So we had-- and we had-- there was no place to run. It was a big valley. So we stayed, and he came down, and he said, you know, you're not allowed to-- to steal potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, look at, we steal potatoes because we hungry. We plain hungry. I said, we are-- they used to call us [NON-ENGLISH], you know, from where we belong, like we're not Russians. He said, no, you have to come to the office, and you were not supposed to steal, and you know, whatever he said.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we both went there. We went there, and the whole village came. You know, they found two thieves. And I said to her, you know, while we were walking-- it was the valley, and then you go up the hill. I said to her, you know, I said, I'm going to talk to him. You hold the potatoes and slowly throw out half of them. I said, throw right and left. So that's what she did. I was talking to his conscience. It didn't help me nothing. But in the meantime, half of the potatoes she throw away, and he didn't see it. He saw it when we came to office, because he saw how much we had. He said, what happened to the potatoes? I said, that's all what we have.

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't prove it. There were a hundred acres of potatoes. You know, this village is-- they have a hundred acres with different kinds of things. So there was a secretary, and they wrote down who we are and what-- and we stole the potatoes. And I said, look at, that's all right. I said, you put us in jail, and we're going to have three meals a day, and we don't have to steal no more. I said, it's time we should relax.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after he was done and wrote down everything, he said, you could have the potatoes and go home. So then we felt so bad. Why we throw away? But when we went home, you know, in the valley, and the wolves were running around. It was about 10 kilometers. It wasn't-- you know. It took time to go up the hill until-- till they made their report and everything. And finally we came home with the few potatoes. And then some rumors came around, you know, somebody from that village steal potatoes there. One asked me. I said, I don't know nothing about it. I said, you know I wouldn't go-- what do I come there?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had-- one time, we cut out a telephone post, because it was cold winter. We have no-- there was no wood, no nothing. So there was a big storm, and a couple of us went, not just me. A couple, we went and we cut out a telephone post, chop it up and had a little fire, just to warm up ourselves. And when it came spring, the mattress was frozen. All three of us slept on one single bed, them two, and I was the middle in the back. So one had to move, all three had to move. And one time, you know, Blasberg, she had lice. And there was no comb. You couldn't buy a comb. And while we were working, they give us some food. There was a lady who used to cook for the workers. And I saw a comb with her, and I stole it. You know, I stole it to comb her out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where we lived, the room, it was one room and then another room, and there was boards. And between the boards were little holes. They weren't connected too good. So she saw when I combed her out, and she went-- and you know, and the rumor was, they stole a comb? But who stole? So she saw we had a comb. They didn't know exactly who did it, but they knew one of us three. So in the meantime, I took the comb and throw it back to her, you know. So you know, it was-- then she-- little by little, I had in mind, it was-- but like I said, in the spring, when we woke up, you know, that mattress was full of ice. It was cold. It was just cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed with a lady. She had three children, and her husband, they took him on Siberia. And she was a bookkeeper. Such a nice, intelligent woman. But the three children, she had nothing to give them to eat, and no-- well, we suffered from the-- Well, we were a little bigger, but the kids were so young. It was pitiful. It was just pitiful. But look, when we came back and we found out what was in concentration camps, we couldn't complain. I mean, at least we were-- we're alive. And we made it through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get paid for the work you did? How did you get--

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to give us a little food. We didn't have no money. No. Just they give us shelter and a little food. But we used to steal a little something and sell it. Like Blasberg, she had the malaria, and she was laid off. And she want a piece of bread. So you know, they used to give like three-quarter of a pound bread, but till you took it in the hand, there was just crumbs, because it didn't stay together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stole some beets, and then there was a different village. It's a-- it was a different type. Like the village we lived, everybody could have a little garden and a little house for themselves. And I went-- I had a lady there, and she used to give me something for that. So one time I went, and on the way, the manager from the village came with a horse and wagon. And you know, like l said, the fields were miles and miles on both sides. And you don't see nobody. And I have full pockets of wheat. And I said, what will they do? And I look, and there was a piece with-- I don't know what you call it. It's-- it's green, and it, you know, it pinches you, and then you get a rash from it. I don't know. It grows. It's a wild wheat. We had it in Europe too. Everybody run away because-- So it was a whole bunch of it. Nobody wanted to touch it because-- so I went in that wheat and hid till he went through, because, you see, it was too-- you know, a mountain. So I saw him. He didn't see me. But when he passed me, then-- and I brought them a little bread. Something. And then when we came here, we don't talk to each other. Oh, that's too bad. How did you get clothing and things like that that you needed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I stole a little. When we lived in the first village, we would work on a vegetable garden, so I used to steal a few tomatoes, a few cucumbers, different things. And I lived not far from the railroad station. And that station went-- the train was going to Kharkov. It took one hour to Kharkov.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to take the vegetables. Wake up real early, when everybody was asleep, hop on the train, and when I used to come to Kharkov, people were so hungry. They used to-- then they pay you whatever you wanted. With the same train, I came back. It took me an hour. So I bought a pair of  shoes. I bought something, you know, from that. But you didn't get money, you know. You have to steal to get something. I-- somehow, I always managed. More like them. They didn't even know what I used to do. Because you afraid. You know, they squeak on you, then you in trouble. So you were able to get clothing and shoes like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: A little, but not-- but most of the time, we were barefooted. The whole summer, we were barefooted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one time, a plane crashed. So from the parachutes, we picked up the material, and we used to make some shoes, whatever, you know. We wear the same thing all the time, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about sanitary conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was water. It wasn't too-- because they didn't have-- there was no electricity. The water, you have to-- from the wells, you know, whatever. You managed. Like I said, we had lice, full of them. And you had no choice. You couldn't even-- you couldn't even buy a-- a comb. I got so swollen up, and everybody thought I'm pregnant. From the food, you know, from malnutrition.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this Blasberg said one time, she said, you know, everybody in the village say they saw you are there a year. And you're so big, you're pregnant, and there is nothing there. And I knew I didn't-- I wasn't pregnant. So we went to a different little town to a clinic. It took a whole day. And then she-- she checked me out. She says, well, you're still a girl. What did you came? I said, I knew I'm a girl, but what-- she said, the weather, you don't eat what you're supposed to eat, you're not clean, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was all swollen up for years. I didn't have my period, nothing. So you know. But like I said, it's still better than to be in concentration camp. I still survived. I was not afraid for my life. And I didn't think too much. See, I always thought my parents would be alive, you know. They were big business people, and we used to deal with Gentiles. I said, somebody will help. I didn't imagine, you know, what happened. So the death thing, you know, it gives you-- And then you're young. I don't know. You could take things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] So but then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after they start to push back the Germans, we went too. Like I said, we always stayed five kilometer, 10 kilometer from the army, behind. I mean, if you have a little brains, you wouldn't do that, but you know, we were so naive, so stupid. But it-- it did a good purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the three years that you were traveling around--

speaker: interviewee
text: Back and forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Based on where the army was.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That's right. Not the one place, you know. Stayed a couple months, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were trying to get back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time. That was-- because people went to Tashkent, to Siberia. You could travel. You didn't need the money. Most of them who survived went there. But we were so naive, we thought tomorrow we go home. That's why we-- what happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the rest of the people that you were evacuated with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? Who knows?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you don't know where they were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You know, we were on our own. There was nobody to tell you what to do. It just was good to have-- I had the paper, and that helped me a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was from your first job with the cows?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were there any-- did you ever do anything religiously while you were traveling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I remember we fast one time. We imagine--

speaker: interviewer
text: Finish your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: We figured out in the Fall. Who know  when it was, but we made up-- we had nothing to eat anyhow, but we made up we're going to-- we didn't-- you know, all the years we were there in Russia, we never met a Jew. We never met a Jew. One time there were-- this Blasberg met-- I don't know who she even met. It was two Jewish soldiers, and they were in the Russian army. But the Russians didn't trust them, so  they worked behind the front line. And they were staying in a different village, so one day, we went to visit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to stay overnight, because it was too far. We walked. You know, it wasn't-- we walked. They were three Jewish men, but they saw, you know, three Jewish girls. They thought we are prostitutes or something. So we spend-- we stayed up a whole night, and the next morning they had to go to work, and we disappeared. And that was for all the time. OK.
